coredata.dc:title: Functionalization of TiO2 with graphene quantum dots for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Xuqiang
Jin, Zhiliang
Xu, Jing
Min, Shixiong
Lu, Gongxuan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) serve as a novel solid-state electron transfer reagent anchored on TiO2 by in situ photo-assisted strategy and greatly enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity in methanol aqueous solution without the noble mental cocatalyst. The excellent photocatalytic activities were ascribed to the GQDs which act as an excellent electron transporters and acceptors, as well as photosensitizer. GQDs not only acted as efficient electron reservoirs and a solid-state electron transfer reagent from the conduction band of TiO2 to GQDs, but also acted as an excellent photosensitizer to sensitize TiO2, in which the photoinduced electrons transfer from excited GQDs to TiO2 to produce H2. In addition, GQDs is nanoscale fragments of graphene which can provide a larger active surface and greatly increase the contact area with the TiO2, which is conducive to rapidly transfer photo-generated electrons due to the large specific area and high carrier mobility of GQDs. Thus, GQDs improved the photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Solid-state electron transfer reagent
TiO2
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sysnthesis of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Photocatalystic activities over GQDs/TiO2 for H2 evolution and stability test
Effect of the different GQDs (wt%) amounts on the photocatalytic activities of GQDs/TiO2
Photocurrent–time and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test
The speculation of mechanism of GQDs/TiO2 for H2 evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the pure TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 samples.
FTIR spectra of the TiO2, GQDs and GQDs/TiO2 samples.

(A) Photocatalystic H2 evolution activities of pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs composites with different amounts of GQDs from 20% (v/v) methanol aqueous solution under UV–Vis light irradiation; (B) Stability test for H2 evolution over TiO2/10% GQDs system in cycle runs.

Effect of the different GQDs (wt%) amounts from 1 to 15% on the photocatalytic activities of GQDs/TiO2 (20 mg) for hydrogen evolution from 20% (v/v) methanol aqueous solution under UV–Vis light irradiation (reaction time: 10 h).
Fluorescence spectra of TiO2, GQDs and GQDs/TiO2 samples in 20% (v/v) methanol aqueous solution.[Catalyst: 0.1 mg/mL].
(A)Transient photocurrent–time profiles of pure TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2; (B) LSV curves of pure TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte under UV–Vis light irradiation. The scan rate was 1 mV s−1.
Schematic illustration for the photocatalytic H2 evolution mechanism over GQDs/TiO2 under UV–Vis light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Functionalization of TiO2 with graphene quantum dots for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Beifang University of Nationalities, Yinchuan 750021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Oxo Synthesis and Selective Oxidation, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Science, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Oxo Synthesis and Selective Oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) serve as a novel solid-state electron transfer reagent anchored on TiO2 by in situ photo-assisted strategy and greatly enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity in methanol aqueous solution without the noble mental cocatalyst. The excellent photocatalytic activities were ascribed to the GQDs which act as an excellent electron transporters and acceptors, as well as photosensitizer. GQDs not only acted as efficient electron reservoirs and a solid-state electron transfer reagent from the conduction band of TiO2 to GQDs, but also acted as an excellent photosensitizer to sensitize TiO2, in which the photoinduced electrons transfer from excited GQDs to TiO2 to produce H2. In addition, GQDs is nanoscale fragments of graphene which can provide a larger active surface and greatly increase the contact area with the TiO2, which is conducive to rapidly transfer photo-generated electrons due to the large specific area and high carrier mobility of GQDs. Thus, GQDs improved the photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) serve as a novel solid-state electron transfer reagent anchored on TiO2 greatly enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. The excellent photocatalytic activities were ascribed to the GQDs which not only serve as efficient electron reservoirs and a solid-state electron transfer reagent to accelerate the electron transfer from the conduction band of TiO2 to GQDs, but also act as an excellent photosensitizer to sensitize TiO2, in which the photoinduced electrons transfer from excited GQDs to TiO2 to produce H2.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen evolution using semiconductor photocatalysts has been considered as an attractive strategy in the conversion of solar energy into chemical energy [1]. Much research has been devoted to develop the semiconductor photocatalysts which were efficiently, economically practical and chemical stability for photocatalytic H2 generation [2]. Among these semiconductor photocatalysts, TiO2 is one of the most attractive photocatalyst materials for photocatalytic H2 evolution due to its low cost, low toxicity and excellent chemical stability [3]. However, because of the large bandgap energy (3.0 eV for rutile and 3.2 eV for anatase), TiO2 can only harvest UV light which take only about 4% of the solar spectrum, resulting in low solar energy conversion efficiency of TiO2 limited [4]. Tremendous efforts have been taken to broaden the absorption spectrum and improve the charge separation efficiency of TiO2, such as doping with C, N, S, Sn, Co, Fe, [5–10] coupling with narrow bandgap semiconductor [11,12], loading with noble metal particles such as Pt and Au nanoparticles [13,14] sensitizing with organic dyes [15–17] and so on. Recently, the TiO2 composite with metal-free carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene [18,19] which can restrain the fast recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes in TiO2 for efficiently enhance photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the carbon nitride polymeric semiconductor (g-C3N4) is also considered to be a promising material to composite with TiO2 for efficient photocatalysis [20,21]. However, further research a high efficiency and low cost strategy to TiO2-based photocatalysts for efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution is necessary.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are generally defined as nanocarbon, have been attracting increasing attention due to its excellent properties, such as strong photoluminescence emission, broadband optical absorption, robust chemical stability, high aqueous solubility and nontoxicity [22–26]. Recently, CQDs have also been studied for photocatalytic H2 generation. It was reported that CQDs as a sensitizer significantly improved photocatalytic activity of TiO2 photoanode for efficient hydrogen generation under visible light [23]. In another reported, CQDs act as an excellent electron reservoir and photosensitizer which modified P25 TiO2 exhibited improved photocatalytic H2 evolution under UV–Vis light irradiation [26].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel class of Zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials consist of single or few-layer graphene with a size less than 100 nm and on behalf of a new type of CQDs with unique properties derived from both graphene and CQDs [27]. GQDs also exhibit excellent optical and electronic properties, and possess a bandgap less than 2.0 eV due to the quantum confinement and edge effects [28]. Unlike the metal-based quantum dots QDs, such as CdS [29], CdTe [30], CdSe [31]and so on, GQDs are nontoxic which is similar to CQDs, not cause contamination of water resources. Various interesting photoelectronic properties of GQDs have been revealed and their potential application in bioimaging [32], sensing [27], solar cells [33] has been explored. However, there is few report about GODs for photocatalytic H2 evolution. It was reported GODs as a green sensitizer to functionalize ZnO nanowire arrays on F-doped SnO2 glass for enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting [28]. Recently, Yu et al. [34] anchored GODs on TiO2 nanotubes array for photoelectrochemical hydrogen production was greatly improved. There is still need to further explore GODs serve as a solid-state electron transfer reagent and photosensitizer which can significantly enhance the H2 evolution activity.

Herein, the water-soluble and well-crystallized GODs were synthesized by a hydrothermal cutting method, which act as a solid-state electron transfer reagent anchored on TiO2 by in situ photo-assisted strategy under UV–Vis light irradiation greatly enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution in methanol aqueous solution without any noble mental cocatalyst, with a comparison to pure TiO2. The excellent photocatalytic activity should be ascribed to the GQDs which act as an excellent electron transporters and acceptors, as well as photosensitizer. The photocatalytic activities of GQDs/TiO2 with different GQDs loading amounts and the electrons transfer behavior of GQDs were investigated. In addition, the corresponding fluorescence results indicated that the charge separation efficiency and photogenerated electrons transfer from TiO2 to the hydrogen evolution active sites was remarkably promoted, and the rang of light-absorption was broaden, thus enhanced the photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution. The mechanism of GQDs improved photocatalytic activity was discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sysnthesis of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs)

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from graphite powder using modified Hummers method [35]. All the reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. In short, 10 g of graphite powder and 5 g of NaNO3 were mixed together, followed the mixture was added to cold concentrated H2SO4 (0 °C, 460 mL) under constant stirring. After 1 h, 30 g of KMnO4 was gradually added to the above solution while keeping the temperature less than 20 °C to prevent overheating and explosion. The mixture was then stirred at 35 °C for 2 h and the resulting solution was diluted with 500 mL of water under vigorous stirring for 15 min. The reaction was terminated by adding distilled water (1.5 L) and then H2O2 solution (25 mL, 30%). The product was filtered, washed repeatedly with HCl solution (1:10, v/v) until sulfate could not be detected with saturated BaCl2 aqueous solution, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 40 °C for 24 h.

Preparation of GQDs follows as literature [36]. Typically, the as-prepared multilayer GO sheets were exfoliated into monolayer GO sheets by ultrasonic for 10 h. Monolayer GO sheets were then thermally reduced into expanded graphene sheets (GSs) at 600 °C for 2 h. GSs (10 mg) were oxidized in concentrated H2SO4 (20 mL) and HNO3 (60 mL) for 18 h under ultrasonic conditions using the ultrasonic cleaner. After the oxidization, the mixture was diluted with deionized water (40 mL), divided into two centrifuge tubes (100 mL), and centrifuged for more than 3 times to remove the acids. The oxidized GSs were dissolved in deionized water (70 mL) with pH tuned to >12 with 7 mL of NH3·H2O and then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (100 mL), maintained at 200 °C for 12 h, and allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The product was filtered through a 0.22 mm microporous membrane. A light-yellow supernatant containing fluorescent GQDs was obtained after the filtered solution was concentrated and centrifuged.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded on a Rigaku B/Max-RB diffractometer with a nickel filtrated Cu Kα radiation operated at 40 kV and 30 mA. The spectra were recorded using a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 100 IR Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Photoluminescence data (PL) were acquired using a FLUOROMAX-4 spectrophotometer at room temperature.

Photocatalytic experiments were conducted in a one-compartment Pyrex cell ca.162 cm3 having a flat window ca.10.2 cm2 for illumination. In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 20 mg of TiO2 catalyst and a certain amount of GQDs were suspended in 100 mL 20% (v/v) methanol aqueous solution with the ultrasound treatment about 10 min. The opening of the cell was sealed with a silicone rubber septum. The reactant mixture was degassed by bubbling N2 gas for 40 min, and then was irradiated by a UV–Vis light for H2 evolution under magnetic stirring condition and measurements of the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. The amount of hydrogen evolution was measured using gas chromatography (Tianmei GC7900, TCD, 13Xcolumn, high-purity N2 as carrier).
The stability testing of H2 evolution over TiO2/10% GQDs was conducted every 10 h for 6 times.
All glassware was rigorously cleaned and carefully rinsed with distilled water prior to use.

The photoelectrochemical measurement was performed on an electrochemical analyzer (PAR VersaSTAT 4) in a homemade standard three-electrode cell. Platinum foil was used as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The working electrodes were prepared by drop-coating homogeneous catalyst suspensions directly onto the precleaned indium tin oxide glass (ITO glass) surface and then dried. The supporting electrolyte was 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The surface area of the working electrode exposed to the electrolyte was about 0.95 cm2. The UV–Vis light was used for excitation.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of pure TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 samples are shown in Fig. 1 . The characteristic diffraction peaks of pure TiO2 shows the anatase TiO2 (JCPDS # 21-1272) and rutile TiO2 phase (JCPDS # 21-1275). The appeared peaks at 25.28°, 36.86°, 37.8°, 48.05°, 53.89°, 55.02°, 62.7°, 70.26°, 74.92° are attributed to (101), (103), (004), (200) (105) (211) (116) (200) and (215) plane of anatase TiO2; and the other peaks at 36.06°, 41.18° and 56.74° are belonged to the (004), (110) and (114) plane of rutile phase TiO2, Which indicating the presence of P25. Notably, there are no different peaks are observed from GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites compared with pure TiO2 and the location and intensity of the diffraction peaks are not change, which clearly indicating the GQDs did not have an impact on TiO2 crystal structure and size. In addition, the XRD patterns of GQDs or graphite oxide is not observed from GQDs/TiO2 samples. On the one hand due to the destruction of the original layered structure of graphite oxide stacking quantum attunement process; on the other hand is due to the graphene quantum dots adding less, so the characteristic diffraction peak disappears.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was also carried out to further characterize the as-obtained GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 2 , the broad absorption band at ∼3432 cm−1 is corresponding to hydrogen bonded OH stretching vibration [37]. The characteristic absorption peaks at ∼3432 cm−1 is assigned to the CH stretching vibration; peaks at ∼1593 cm−1 and ∼1416 cm−1 are related to OCO asymmetric and symmetric vibrations; the peaks at ∼1090 cm−1 and ∼990 cm−1 existed are related to the CO bonds and epoxy groups; and the broad absorption bands at 480–700 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of TiOTi and TiOC vibrations [38]. The FTIR results indicating that GQDs have been successfully attached to the TiO2 nanoparticles, which is in agreement with the XRD results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalystic activities over GQDs/TiO2 for H2 evolution and stability test
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A shows the photocatalystic H2 evolution activities of pure TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites from methanol aqueous solution at room temperature under UV–Vis light irradiation. The pure TiO2 exhibits a relatively low photocatalystic activity for H2 evolution and it only 18.74 μmol of H2 produced after 10 h irradiation, suggesting that the pure TiO2 itself was inactive for H2 evolution probably due to its rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [37]. The photocatalytic H2 evolution activities were greatly improved with the GODs added. The amounts of H2 was 41.26 μmol over the GQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst, which was 2.20 times higher than the pure TiO2 under the same reaction conditions. These results indicated that GODs is a key factor to improve H2 evolution activity of pure TiO2 because of its larger surface area, high carrier mobility and a unique zero-dimensional effect, it can efficiently transfer electrons and inhibit the photogenerated electron-hole pairs recombination effectively. In addition, the favorable energy levels between the GQDs and TiO2 that induce directional transport charge was also considered to promote the efficiency of charge separation.

Furthermore, the stability test of GQDs/TiO2-10% photocatalyst was carried out and the hydrogen production ability of the system was measured within 60 h. The system replaced once every 10 h, the results shown in Fig. 3B. The GQDs/TiO2 exhibits high stability for hydrogen production in cycling runs under UV–Vis light irradiation. The system can maintain stable in first five times cycle. The hydrogen production ability of the sixth cycle was declined slightly, but not significantly, which maybe the GQDs was dissolved partly when the long time reaction in the aqueous solution. These results show that the addition of GQDs can significantly promote the hydrogen yield and stable performance, which can be attributed to the excellent electron transport properties and the quantum confinement effect of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the different GQDs (wt%) amounts on the photocatalytic activities of GQDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the effect of different GQDs (wt%) in GQDs/TiO2 on the H2 evolution activities was investigated and an inconspicuous impact from the GQDs content was found. As shown in Fig. 4 , When the GQDs added, the photocatalytic H2 evolution activities were gradually improved with the GQDs content raise. The amount of H2 was 33.59, 34.15 and 35.25 μmol when the weight ratio of GQDs from 1%, 3% and 5%.The maximum amount of H2 production up to 41.26 μmol when 10 wt% GQDs added. The reason maybe GQDs is nanoscale fragments of grapheme which can provide a larger active surface and greatly increase the contact area with the TiO2, which is conducive to rapidly transfer photo-generated electrons. Thus, the photocatalytic activity of GQDs/TiO2 remarkably enhanced for H2 production.

In order to prove the important role of GQDs in facilitating the transfer of photogenerated electrons to improve the photocatalytic activity of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, the photoluminescence quenching of TiO2, GQDs and P25/GQDs were further examined. As shown in Fig. 5 , TiO2 aqueous solution showed a typically extensive emission peak at 396 nm. When the GQDs was added, a clearly decline occurred, indicating that the photo-generated electron-hole pairs recombination of TiO2 is greatly suppressed and effectively enhanced the electron transfer due to the superior electron transport properties of GQDs. In addition, an obvious red shift of the intrinsic excited peak in the GQDs/TiO2 with respect to the pure TiO2, which can be attributed to the strong interaction between the GQDs and TiO2 and formation of the of TiOC bond and the well-known quantum confinement effect by shifting the conduction and valence band edges in opposite directions. Furthermore, it suggested that the GQDs can effectively broaden the photosensitizing band of TiO2. Thus, it accounts for the significantly enhanced H2 production activity when the GQDs addition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocurrent–time and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further confirm the photocatalytic electron transfer mechanism, the transient photocurrent responses of pure TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 coated on ITO were investigated for several on-off cycles of intermittent irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the ITO/GQDs/TiO2 electrode shows a stable photocurrent and a higher photocurrent intensity with respect to the ITO/TiO2 electrode was observed, which is agreement with their photocatalytic H2 production activity. The photocurrent intensity of ITO/GQDs/TiO2 can reach 1.7 mA/cm2 at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl which is much higher than 0.8 mA/cm2 of ITO/TiO2 electrode. The significantly enhanced photocurrent response of GQDs/TiO2 can be attributed to the coupling with GQDs which act as a solid-state electronic transfer reagent to trap the photoinduced electrons from TiO2 and thus enhance the separation efficiency of electrons and holes. In addition, the π conjugated GQDs may serve as a photosensitizer, like organic dyes, and the photoexcited electrons transfer from the GQDs to TiO2 [28], thereby improve the efficiency of photocatalytic H2 production. This result indicates that the GQDs play an important role for the electron transfer efficiency and inhibition the photogenerated electron - hole pair recombination of GQDs/TiO2.

Furthermore, to get further information about the transport behaviors of the photoinduced charge carries, the electrochemical H2 evolution activities of pure TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 nanohybrid deposited on ITO glass were investigated using the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) technique. Fig. 6B shows the cathodic current of bare ITO electrode belonged to the reduction of water to H2 which was extremely low even at high applied potentials. The cathodic currents of ITO/TiO2 and ITO/GQDs/TiO2 electrode had a significant improvement beyond −0.23 V. And the highest current density was observed from ITO/GQDs/TiO2 electrode, which proved that ITO/GQDs/TiO2 was a remarkable co-catalyst which can efficiently catalyze the reduction of water to H2. The reason maybe GQDs is nanoscale fragments of grapheme which can provide a larger active surface and greatly increase the contact area with the TiO2, which is conducive to rapidly transfer photo-generated electrons. This result further testify GQDs act as solid-state electron transfer reagent can accelerate the photoinduced electrons transfer, and indicates that GQDs/TiO2 is an excellent electric-catalytic material for hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The speculation of mechanism of GQDs/TiO2 for H2 evolution
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic H2 evolution over GQDs/TiO2 system can be explained in terms of Fig. 7 . The GQDs in GQDs/TiO2 act as a solid-state electronic transfer reagent and photosensitizer for significantly enhanced the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. Under UV–Vis light irradiation, the coupled GQDs on the surface of TiO2 serve as the electron reservoirs and solid-state electron transfer reagent to trap photoinduced electrons from the conduction band of TiO2, which is beneficial to the efficient separation of electrons and holes, and thus enhanced the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. In addition, the π conjugated GQDs can also be excited by absorbing photons of light which may act as a photosensitizer, like organic dyes, to sensitize TiO2, and then transfer photo-excited electrons from the GQDs to the conduction band of TiO2 to produce H2, thereby improving the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity.

In summary, we have explored the use of GQDs as an efficient solid-state electronic transfer medium to build unique photocatalytic systems of GQDs/TiO2 by in situ photo-assisted strategy for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from water under UV–Vis light irradiation. GQDs not only serve as efficient electron reservoirs and solid-state electron transfer reagent to accelerate the electron transfer from the conduction band of TiO2 to GQDs, but also act as an excellent photosensitizer to sensitize TiO2, in which the photoinduced electrons transfer from excited GQDs to TiO2 to produce H2. Thereby GQDs enhanced the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity, and further confirmed the feasibility of GQDs as efficient solid-state electronic transfer medium for photocatalytic water splitting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (21263001, 21463001 and 21433007).
